title: Reconstruction as a Pretext Task
concept: MAE uses reconstruction of raw pixel values in masked patches as its pre-training pretext task, optimizing with Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the input sequence length for the encoder in MAE compared to the original patch sequence?

options.text: It is reduced by 75% (only unmasked patches are processed).
It is doubled to add padding.

exercises.explain: Correct! Only the 25% visible patches enter the encoder block, saving substantial compute.

cards.title: The reconstruction challenge
cards.body: If you ERASURE 75% of a photo, how do you train the model to paint it back? We need a decoder network that takes the encoder's clues and outputs raw pixels for the missing pieces. We then compare the model's drawing with the original photo to grade its accuracy.

cards.title: Pixel Reconstruction
cards.body: The pretext task in MAE is pixel reconstruction. The model must predict the raw RGB pixel values for the hidden patches. The model is trained by calculating the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss between the predicted pixels and the ground-truth pixels, computed strictly on the masked patches.

cards.title: Restoring a fresco
cards.body: Imagine a historical painting that has decayed, leaving only a few clean spots. An art restorer (the model) must paint in the missing details. We check their accuracy by comparing their restoration with a photo of the original painting. The closer the match, the better the restorer is.

cards.title: Decoder outputs
cards.body: For a 16x16 patch, the decoder outputs a vector of length 768 (16 x 16 x 3). The model reshapes this vector back into a 2D color patch and pastes it into the missing grid slot for evaluation.

cards.title: Not aiming for photographic perfection
cards.body: You might think the goal is to generate beautiful, photorealistic images. In fact, MAE outputs are often slightly blurry. The goal isn't art; it is to force the **encoder** to learn rich semantic representations that we can transfer to other tasks.

cards.title: MAE Reconstruction Pipeline
cards.body: How data flows through the reconstruction task:

left.text:
75% patches removed
25% visible sent to encoder
Creates a visual puzzle

right.text:
All patches output by decoder
MSE computed on masked slots
Forces semantic understanding

exercises.prompt: Which loss function is typically used to optimize the pixel reconstruction task in MAE?
exercises.explain: MAE computes MSE loss between the predicted and original pixel values of the masked patches.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The reconstruction loss is calculated over all patches in the image, including the visible ones.
exercises.explain: False. To focus training, MAE computes MSE loss strictly on the masked (hidden) patch coordinates.
The output vector is reshaped back into a 2D square (e.g. 16x16x3) to match the original image patch layout.
exercises.sentence: The decoder outputs a flat vector of pixel values that is reshaped back into a ___ patch.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary objective of the reconstruction pretext task?
exercises.explain: Predicting missing regions requires the encoder to learn semantic visual features that generalize to other downstream tasks.

options.text: To force the encoder to learn general visual features by predicting missing data
To generate highly polished artwork for production apps
To compress the image size by 10x

exercises.prompt: Why does reconstructuring raw pixels work well as a pretext task for transformers, whereas it historically struggled for CNNs?

options.text: Transformers can route global information from distant visible patches to fill blanks, whereas local CNN kernels struggle to span large masked gaps.
Because CNNs cannot compute MSE loss.
Because transformers automatically convert all images to text.
Because CNNs have too many attention heads.

exercises.explain: Brilliant! Transformer self-attention bridges large gaps easily by pulling clues from the entire image, making it perfect for MAE. Next: the asymmetric encoder-decoder design!
